ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-0: 〜なのに marks emotional contrast between a recent action and surprising consequence. Fits here because buying the computer recently makes its obsolescence emotionally surprising. Unlike のに (purpose), which shows intent, this なのに emphasizes the unexpectedness.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-1: 〜なのに expresses surprising contradiction: information *should be* current, but *isn't*. Conveys emotional disappointment at the mismatch. Differs from 反面 (neutral comparison) and にしては (qualifying judgment).
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-2: 〜なのに shows contrast between expectation and reality—new shoes shouldn't need repair, yet they do. Signals emotional surprise at contradiction. のに (purpose) wouldn't work because the shoes don't exist *in order to* be repaired.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-3: 〜なのに conveys surprising conclusion: because you walked, your legs must be strong. The particle marks emotional inevitability of the result. としても (even if) would be hypothetical rather than conclusive.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-4: 〜なのに marks the emotional surprise of discovering something unexpected—Jim waking alone. The particle signals that the circumstance contradicts expectation. のに alone lacks the emotional emphasis.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-1: 〜と marks automatic, natural consequence: leaving home *automatically triggers* rain. Shows inevitable sequence, not hypothesis. Unlike ば (hypothetical condition), と implies the result naturally follows.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-2: 〜と shows natural consequence of a condition: *if* it's here, *then* you can buy a TV at this price—a predictable outcome. なら would be given-situation hypothetical; と marks cause-and-effect inevitability.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-3: 〜と marks seasonal inevitability: when winter arrives, rain naturally follows. The particle expresses automatic timing, not hypothetical if-then. としても would incorrectly suggest permission/concession.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-4: 〜と marks obligatory consequence: arriving at Tachikawa station *automatically triggers* the need to call. Shows natural cause-effect chain. なら would weaken to hypothetical obligation.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-0: のに marks purpose/means: studying Japanese *in order to* progress takes time. Explains why effort is needed. Unlike contrast-marked なのに, this のに shows intentional goal-oriented action.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-1: のに expresses purpose: asking what time/place works *in order to* meet you. The particle links the verb to its intended goal. Differs from なのに (emotional contrast) and にしては (qualifying judgment).
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-2: のに marks necessity for purpose: a passport is required *in order to* travel overseas. Links object to its functional goal. なのに would incorrectly signal emotional contradiction.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-3: のに expresses function: swimwear is used *for* (in order to) swim. Shows intended purpose of an object. は (contrast) and なのに (emotional contrast) don't convey functional purpose.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-4: のに marks time-to-purpose: it took five months *in order to* finish the job. Shows duration needed to achieve a goal. にしては would imply unexpected qualification, not purpose.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-0: 〜は marks contrastive topic: I don't eat meat [but I eat fish]. The particle singles out meat for negation while implying opposite for others. のに wrongly signals emotional surprise.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-1: 〜は emphasizes contrast within a statement: she's alive [but implicitly not well]. The particle isolates the property for contrast. としても (even if) would be hypothetical, not contrastive.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-2: 〜は marks topical contrast: going to baseball games—I like that [but dislike other things]. The particle frames the topic for implicit comparison. のに wrongly signals emotional contradiction.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-3: 〜は contrasts two properties of the same subject: I like addition *but* dislike subtraction. Each particle isolates a property for direct comparison. なのに would imply emotional surprise.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-4: 〜は marks contrastive subject: he was living in Shanghai [implying the war's context differed for him]. The particle spotlights his location for contrast with the war's setting. のに wouldn't work contextually.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-0: 〜反面 means 'on the other hand,' marking structural contrast between two sides. Brief phrase shown as example of the pattern. Neutral comparison unlike emotional なのに.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-1: 〜反面 balances opposing qualities: brother is kind *on the other hand* he's mean. Links two contrasting aspects without emotional judgment. のに would wrongly suggest surprise.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-2: 〜反面 contrasts benefits and drawbacks: the job pays well *on the other hand* requires long hours. Structurally weighs two sides. としても would be hypothetical rather than comparative.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-3: 〜反面 balances opposing properties: cloth resists water *on the other hand* weak to heat. Compares two contrasting characteristics neutrally. のに would add emotional surprise element.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-4: 〜反面 presents dual perspective: internet aids learning *on the other hand* exposes children to harm. Weighs benefits vs. risks structurally. なのに would incorrectly emphasize emotional contradiction.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-0: 〜にしては marks surprising qualification: for an old person, he looks young—unexpected given his age. The particle judges something against a standard. のに would express emotional contrast differently.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-1: 〜にしては qualifies with irony: considering he's wealthy, he drives an old car—incongruent. Evaluates one fact against another as context. は (contrast topic) doesn't do this qualification.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-2: 〜にしては marks unexpectedness for a season: June morning that's cold—surprising *for* that month. Judges temperature against seasonal norm. のに would differently frame emotional surprise.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-3: 〜にしては qualifies against age: a 70-year-old with surprising vigor—unexpected *for* his age. The particle judges the consequent against the standard. にしては differs from neutral 反面.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-4: 〜にしては marks local anomaly: hotel is expensive *for* this town's context. Judges price against the location's standard. のに would wrongly signal emotional contradiction.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-0: 〜としても marks concessive condition: even *if* I could marry her, it won't happen soon. Allows premise and contradicts expectation. なら would be straightforward conditional, not concessive.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-1: 〜としても concedes a point: even *if* parents are famous, talent is required for recognition. Acknowledges condition while negating consequence. のに would add emotional surprise element.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-2: 〜としても concedes hierarchy: even *if* someone is a subordinate, gratitude is proper—principle overrides status. Allows premise to soften duty. のに would differently frame surprise.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-3: 〜としても marks potential role: Linux advancing *even as* a game OS—acknowledges the unlikely context. Concedes application while affirming progress. のに wouldn't express this conditional allowance.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-4: 〜としても concedes quality: even *if* he's nice, trust isn't guaranteed—admits positive while reserving judgment. Allows premise without endorsing conclusion. なのに would signal emotional contradiction.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-0: のに expresses regret/wish in conditional: I wish today's weather matched yesterday's—emotional unfulfilled desire. Marks counterfactual longing. としても would be hypothetical permission rather than regret.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-1: のに expresses wish in conditional: 'If only rain would stop!'—marks emotional desire for changed circumstance. Conveys regret at reality. なのに would wrongly emphasize contrast rather than longing.
